questions.question: What is the difference between val and var?
questions.title: What is the difference between val and var?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, val and var are used to declare variables, but they differ in mutability.

sections.heading: val (Immutable)
items: The value cannot be changed once assigned.
Helps write safer, predictable code.
Example:

sections.heading: val (Immutable)
sections.code:
val name = "Aasim"
      // name = "John" // ❌ Error

sections.heading: var (Mutable)
items: The value can be reassigned anytime.
Useful when the variable needs to change during program execution.
Example:

sections.heading: var (Mutable)
sections.code:
var age = 25
      age = 26 // ✅ Allowed

questions.question: What is the advantage of using const in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is the advantage of using const in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, const is used to declare compile-time constants. These are immutable values known at compile time, offering several advantages:

sections.heading: Advantages
items: Performance – Since values are determined at compile time, accessing them is faster than regular val.
Immutability – Ensures the value cannot be changed anywhere in the code.
Global Access – Can be used at the top level or in object declarations, making them accessible without creating an instance.
Cleaner Code – Ideal for defining constants like URLs, keys, or fixed numbers.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
const val BASE_URL = "https://api.example.com/"
const val MAX_RETRY = 3

questions.question: When to use lateinit and how to check if it has been initialized?
questions.title: When to use lateinit and how to check if it has been initialized?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: lateinit is used to declare non-null variables without initializing them immediately. It is commonly used for dependencies or properties that will be initialized later, like Android views or injected objects.

sections.heading: When to Use lateinit
items: For non-null var properties that cannot be initialized in the constructor.
Common in Android for views:

sections.heading: When to Use lateinit
sections.code: lateinit var textView: TextView

sections.heading: How to Check Initialization
items: Use ::variableName.isInitialized to verify before accessing it:

sections.heading: How to Check Initialization

questions.question: How to do lazy initialization of variables in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to do lazy initialization of variables in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Lazy initialization allows you to initialize a variable only when it is accessed for the first time, improving performance and saving resources.

sections.heading: How to Use lazy
items: Use the lazy delegate for read-only (val) properties.

sections.heading: How to Use lazy
How to Use lazy
items: The block runs only once, on the first access of name.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: lazy is thread-safe by default.
Ideal for expensive operations or values not needed immediately.
Only works with val, not var.

questions.question: What is an init block in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is an init block in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The init block in Kotlin is an initializer block that runs when an instance of a class is created. It is used to execute code or initialize properties during object creation.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Can have multiple init blocks; they execute in the order defined.
Runs after primary constructor parameters are passed.
Often used for validation, logging, or complex property initialization.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: Explain the difference between == and === in Kotlin.
questions.title: Explain the difference between == and === in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, == and === are used for comparison, but they check different things.

sections.heading: == (Structural Equality)
items: Checks if values are equal.
Calls the equals() method internally.
Example:

sections.heading: == (Structural Equality)
sections.code:
val a = "Hello"
val b = "Hello"
println(a == b) // ✅ true

sections.heading: === (Referential Equality)
items: Checks if both references point to the same object in memory.
Example:

sections.heading: === (Referential Equality)
sections.code:
val a = "Hello"
val b = "Hello"
println(a === b) // ✅ true or false depending on memory reference

questions.question: What are companion objects in Kotlin?
questions.title: What are companion objects in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A companion object is a singleton inside a class that holds class-level members, similar to Java's static.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Declared with companion object inside a class.
Can contain constants, factory methods, utilities.
Accessed without creating an instance.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What is the equivalent of Java static methods in Kotlin?
questions.title: What is the equivalent of Java static methods in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin doesn't have static methods. You can achieve similar functionality using:

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Companion Object – Class-level functions accessed without an instance:

sections.heading: Introduction
Introduction
items: Top-Level Functions – Functions outside a class, accessible directly:

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code:
fun greet() = println("Hello")
greet()

questions.question: How to create a Singleton class in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to create a Singleton class in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Kotlin, creating a singleton is simple using the object keyword. This ensures only one instance of the class exists.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: object creates a thread-safe singleton automatically.
No need for private constructor or getInstance() like in Java.
Can contain properties, methods, and initialization code.

questions.question: Explain open, abstract, final, internal, public, and private keywords in Kotlin.
questions.title: Explain open, abstract, final, internal, public, and private keywords in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
items: open – Class or function can be inherited or overridden.
abstract – Class or function must be implemented in a subclass; cannot be instantiated.
final – Default modifier; cannot be inherited or overridden.
public – Default visibility; accessible from anywhere.
private – Accessible within the same class only.
internal – Accessible within the same module.

questions.question: What is a data class in Kotlin and what methods are auto-generated?
questions.title: What is a data class in Kotlin and what methods are auto-generated?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A data class in Kotlin is a class designed to hold data. It automatically generates useful methods, reducing boilerplate code.

sections.heading: Auto-Generated Methods
items: equals() – Checks equality based on property values.
hashCode() – Generates hash code based on properties.
toString() – Returns a string representation of the object.
copy() – Creates a copy of the object with optional property changes.
componentN() – Functions for destructuring declarations.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)

val user1 = User("Aasim", 25)
val user2 = user1.copy(age = 26)
println(user1) // Output: User(name=Aasim, age=25)

questions.question: Explain inline classes (value classes) in Kotlin.
questions.title: Explain inline classes (value classes) in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Inline classes (value classes) wrap a single value without creating an extra object at runtime, improving performance.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Declared with @JvmInline value class.
Can have one property and methods.
Implements interfaces if needed.

sections.heading: Example
sections.content: `kotlin @JvmInline value class UserId(val id: String) fun printId(userId: UserId) { println(userId.id) } printId(UserId("123")) // Output: 123

questions.question: What is the purpose of inline, noinline, crossinline, and reified keywords?
questions.title: What is the purpose of inline, noinline, crossinline, and reified keywords?

sections.heading: inline, noinline, crossinline, and reified in Kotlin
items: inline – Inlines the function at the call site to reduce lambda overhead.
noinline – Prevents a lambda parameter of an inline function from being inlined.
crossinline – Prevents non-local returns in a lambda passed to an inline function.
reified – Allows access to a generic type at runtime in an inline function.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What are higher-order functions and how do you return a function from a function?
questions.title: What are higher-order functions and how do you return a function from a function?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A higher-order function takes a function as a parameter or returns a function.
sections.code:
fun operate(a: Int, b: Int, op: (Int, Int) -> Int) = op(a, b)
val sum = operate(5, 3) { x, y -> x + y } // 8

sections.heading: Example: Returning a Function

questions.question: What are lambda expressions in Kotlin?
questions.title: What are lambda expressions in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A lambda expression is an anonymous function that can be assigned, passed, or returned.

questions.question: What are extension functions and how do you use them?
questions.title: What are extension functions and how do you use them?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Extension functions allow you to add new functions to existing classes without inheriting or modifying them.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Can be called like regular methods on the class.
Does not actually modify the original class.
Useful for enhancing library or built-in classes safely.

questions.question: Explain scope functions (let, apply, run, with, also) and their use-cases.
questions.title: Explain scope functions (let, apply, run, with, also) and their use-cases.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Scope functions provide a temporary scope for an object, improving readability and conciseness.

sections.heading: Introduction
items: let – it as context; good for null checks or chaining.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code: name?.let { println(it.length) }

sections.heading: Introduction
items: run – this as context; returns lambda result; for computations.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code: val result = "Hello".run { length + 5 }

sections.heading: Introduction
items: with – this as context; called on object; for grouping operations.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code: with(builder) { append("Hello"); append("World") }

sections.heading: Introduction
items: apply – this as context; returns object; for object initialization.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code: val person = Person().apply { name="Aasim"; age=25 }

sections.heading: Introduction
items: also – it as context; returns object; for side-effects.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code: val numbers = mutableListOf(1,2,3).also { println(it) }

questions.question: Difference between apply and with, and when to use each.
questions.title: Difference between apply and with, and when to use each.

sections.heading: apply
items: Called on an object, returns the object.
Use for object initialization.

sections.heading: apply
sections.code: val person = Person().apply { name="Aasim"; age=25 }

sections.heading: with
items: Takes an object as parameter, returns lambda result.
Use for grouping operations or computing values.

sections.heading: with
sections.code: val length = with("Hello") { println(this); length }

questions.question: What are labels in Kotlin and how do you use them?
questions.title: What are labels in Kotlin and how do you use them?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Labels let you control flow in nested loops or lambdas using break, continue, or return.

sections.heading: Example
items: break@label – exit a specific loop
continue@label – continue a specific loop
return@label – return from a lambda or function

questions.question: Explain sealed classes and common use-cases in Android.
questions.title: Explain sealed classes and common use-cases in Android.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Sealed classes represent restricted class hierarchies where all possible subclasses are known at compile time.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Declared using sealed class.
All subclasses must be defined in the same file.
Useful with when expressions because it ensures exhaustive checks (no else needed).

sections.heading: Common Android Use-Cases
items: UI State Handling – Loading, Success, Error states.
API Response Wrappers – Success, Failure, Loading results.
Navigation Events – Different navigation actions.
ViewModel State Management – Represent screen states clearly.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: Difference between List and Array types in Kotlin.
questions.title: Difference between List and Array types in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: List

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Part of Kotlin Collections.
Usually immutable (listOf()).
Dynamic size.
Provides many built-in utility functions.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Array

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Fixed size once created.
Mutable (elements can be changed).
Created using arrayOf().
Closer to low-level memory structure.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Use List → When you need flexible, collection-based operations. Use Array → When size is fixed or performance/memory control is needed.

questions.question: How to remove duplicates from an array or collection in Kotlin?
questions.title: How to remove duplicates from an array or collection in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Use distinct() → Returns list with unique elements.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code:
val list = listOf(1, 2, 2, 3, 3)
val unique = list.distinct()

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Use toSet() → Removes duplicates by converting to Set.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code: val unique = list.toSet()

sections.heading: Introduction
items: For Array

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.code:
val arr = arrayOf(1, 2, 2, 3)
val unique = arr.distinct()

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: distinct() keeps order toSet() removes duplicates but may not guarantee order

questions.question: Explain Delegates (lazy, observable, vetoable) in Kotlin.
questions.title: Explain Delegates (lazy, observable, vetoable) in Kotlin.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Delegates allow you to delegate property behavior to another object.

sections.heading: lazy
items: Initializes value only when accessed first time.
Useful for expensive object creation.

sections.heading: lazy
sections.code: val data by lazy { loadData() }

sections.heading: observable
items: Triggers callback after value changes.
Useful for tracking or updating UI.

sections.heading: observable
vetoable
items: Allows validation before value changes.
Change happens only if condition returns true.

sections.heading: vetoable

questions.question: What are coroutines in Kotlin?
questions.title: What are coroutines in Kotlin?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Coroutines are a lightweight concurrency feature in Kotlin used to perform asynchronous and non-blocking tasks in a simple and efficient way.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Help run background tasks like API calls, database operations, etc.
More efficient than traditional threads.
Use suspend functions to pause and resume execution without blocking the main thread.
Work with CoroutineScope, Dispatchers, and Jobs for lifecycle and threading control.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: Difference between suspending and blocking functions.
questions.title: Difference between suspending and blocking functions.

sections.heading: Suspending Functions
items: Use the suspend keyword.
Pause execution without blocking the thread.
Allow other tasks to run while waiting (efficient).
Mainly used with coroutines.

sections.heading: Suspending Functions
sections.content: Example

sections.heading: Blocking Functions
items: Block the current thread until the task finishes.
Prevent other tasks from running on that thread.
Can cause UI freezes if used on the main thread.

sections.heading: Blocking Functions
sections.content: Example
